All right, the accountability paradox. What is this paradox? It is the in and the yang of how hard you hold someone accountable to a project, task, deadline, when there are obvious outside factors in flow-saving delivery. Do you blame it on them or do you blame it on circumstance? Who's accountable? So I want to start with an example. Say your salesperson has a quota of selling 20 challenges this week. A salesperson comes to you on Wednesday and shows you that there's a bunch of black ice, these roads are closed, and nobody wants to drive, right? It's like terrible, it's all over the weather, etc. So nobody wants to show up to their point. The salesperson then proceeds to say they can close 20 people in nine business days rather than six. Do you hold her accountable for missing her numbers or do you affirm her for readjusting her goal to be more realistic? Right? So if you prosecute her for readjusting her goal, you're probably going to discourage her from confronting you in the future, which can then lead to a decrease in her performance, right? Because there's lack of trust, lack of transparency. But if you don't hold her accountable, then people who are meeting their goals will feel resentful and stupid. They'll be like, why did I work so hard to meet my goals if this bozo missed her deadline? She can't even do this crap, right? So there are two assumptions. Okay? One, do you assume your employees are creative, intelligent, and motivated? Answer that in your head. Or do you believe your employees are lazy, conniving, and counting down the minutes until they leave every day? Okay? You just start with this in mind. If you believe they are lazy, you might as well give up on the team ever expressing any creativity or any deviation from the plan because you will not get it. Because it's always better to assume that people have good intentions unless they prove you otherwise. Because the moment you treat people like they have bad intentions, that's the moment they develop bad intentions. Okay? And so regardless, you still need to hold them accountable so you don't look like a dead, right? Because it's like, even if there are existential circumstances, you still need to hold someone accountable. So I want to talk about the three dynamics of accountability so you can understand what you have to do in these situations. Okay? Number one is accounting for effort. Okay? This one's easy. In order to become incredibly successful or world class at anything, you need extraordinary effort. Okay? And so if someone is not getting it, that is when they need to be checked. So if you feel that that person has exhausted all options before adjusting their goal, before adjusting that deadline, before not completing that task, if you feel they truly went the extra mile to exhaust their options, then you can check this off and say, okay, well, they put in the effort, right? Effort is number one. Number two is promises. Okay? So most successful organizations have external and internal promises. Internal meaning how you operate your team and then external meaning how you operate with your clients, right? These are promises that regarding, they could regard speed, value, quality and time delivery. Okay? Holding people to these promises is crucial to getting things done. Okay? Because if you don't hold them to the promises, the promises become meaningless and therefore there are no promises and everything you speak is false, right? So some promises are easier to keep than others. What you need to do is decide which have many factors and parties involved to be upheld and which are hard promises. Okay? So is there a promise that has too many factors involved? There are too many parties involved. It's too difficult to complete that you cannot uphold that promise and then which ones are easier to keep and upheld, right? So when you're looking at what somebody fell short of when it comes to a promise that you have with your customers or with your team, was it that it was too complicated? There were too many other parties involved and you need to adjust the promise or is it that the promise is easy and again it was lack of effort, right? Results, okay? This is the hardest part of accountability because someone fails to deliver a result. He or she promised for, is clearly responsible for, then it's more complicated and therefore it needs to be filtered through a couple of different lenses, okay? So we want to figure out why did they not attain that result? Number one, seniority and track record. Number two, degree of difficulty and number three is amount of stupid, okay? So I'm going to explain these. Number one, being seniority and track record, okay? You should expect more from someone who has been at the company longer, adding higher level position because they are better at decision making and forecasting or at least you should expect that of them. You should expect someone in a senior position, in a higher level position, to have the knowledge expertise to make better judgments from the beginning, to do better forecasting and to set better goals for themselves, right? That's one. Two is degree of difficulty. Some things are just really hard to do, okay? And we cannot, there's no way around it, right? So an example would be hitting a sales quota during a recession, okay? When you're deciding the consequence of missing that goal, take into account the difficulty beyond your realm of control. You can control a recession, right? But they can do their darn best to hit a goal and then maybe that goal needs to be readjusted due to outside circumstances, okay? And then number three, amount of stupid, okay? So you don't want to punish someone for taking a risk because that will discourage them from ever taking a risk again, but you also don't want to reward them for taking like a stupid risk, okay? So if someone missed the result, did they take an obviously stupid risk? Did they neglect to consider the consequences if they didn't hit the goal and if the risk went toward the negative side, okay? Or were they smart and the risks just didn't work out in their favor and they did their best, they put the effort in, they made correct promises and just didn't work out, okay? Overall, it's a balance between letting people take a risk and then holding them too tightly accountable, okay? So accountability is important and you need to hold people accountable, but it's not the only thing to consider. So I suggest that you go through these slides multiple times and that you have your leaders do so as well so you can understand how to come up with your own way of viewing each situation because every situation is different and there's always different outside circumstances coming in and the last thing you want to do is be too hard one way or the other.
